title: Phosphorus Runoff and Eutrophication
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive p5.js simulation showing how fertilizer rates drive phosphorus runoff, algal blooms, oxygen depletion, and fish kills with buffer strip mitigation

learningObjectives: Predict the downstream effects of excess phosphorus input on aquatic ecosystems
Explain the eutrophication cascade from nutrient loading through algal bloom to hypoxia
Evaluate the effectiveness of buffer strips as a mitigation strategy for agricultural runoff
prerequisites: Understanding of the phosphorus cycle basics
Familiarity with aquatic ecosystem food webs
Introduction to water quality and dissolved oxygen

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Cause-and-effect simulation with controlled variable manipulation
keyQuestions: Why is there a time delay between phosphorus input and fish kills?
At what fertilizer level does the lake ecosystem reach a tipping point?
How do buffer strips reduce phosphorus reaching waterways?
Why is phosphorus considered the limiting nutrient in most freshwater systems?
How does this simulation connect to real-world events like the Gulf of Mexico dead zone?
commonMisconceptions: Algal blooms are caused by the algae being toxic -- the primary problem is oxygen depletion when algae die and decompose, not the algae themselves
More fertilizer always means more crop production -- beyond optimal levels, excess fertilizer runs off without benefit to crops
Eutrophication effects are immediate -- there is a significant time lag between nutrient input and ecosystem collapse
assessmentOpportunities: Have students identify the tipping point in fertilizer levels using their experimental data
Ask students to design a comprehensive runoff prevention plan for a farm near a lake
Connect simulation results to the Lake Erie or Gulf of Mexico dead zone case studies
